element_introduction_order.mastery_context: spatial awareness of empty squares and piece placement
understanding piece ownership and color assignment
recognizing standard board state and initial player positions
understanding how pieces bounded in straight lines are turned over
understanding player rotation and pass rule
counting final disks to determine winner

typical_relief_triggers: securing board control in key areas
typical_tension_triggers: limited available move options (approaching pass state)
opponent flanking multiple pieces in one move
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: gradual escalation: early game has many move options, mid-game involves strategic positioning, late-game narrows to forced moves with high consequence

difficulty_curve.early_game: low difficulty; multiple valid moves available; board is sparse and capture opportunities are straightforward
difficulty_curve.mid_game: moderate difficulty; move options remain available but consequences become more complex; piece flanking involves multiple directions
difficulty_curve.late_game: high difficulty; board fills up, move options dwindle, single moves have cascading territorial consequences, possible pass states force strategic planning
notable_spikes: endgame when few empty squares remain and each placement triggers cascading flips
situations where player faces pass (no legal moves available)

notable_level_patterns.description: Game always begins with identical setup (4 disks in center square, alternating light/dark). This removes variability and focuses skill on mid-game and endgame strategy rather than opening novelty.
Single moves can expand player's controlled territory significantly through chaining flips in multiple directions. Dominant strategy shifts from early expansion to late-game containment.
Endgame often presents situations where limited empty squares force specific move sequences with predetermined outcomes, reducing player agency but increasing calculation depth.
